blocks.content:
Comparison operators compare values and return `true` or `false`.

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|----------|---------|----------|
| == | Loosely equal | 5 == '5' returns true |
| === | Strictly equal | 5 === '5' returns false |
| != | Not equal | 5 != 3 returns true |
| !== | Strictly not equal | 5 !== '5' returns true |
| > | Greater than | 10 > 5 returns true |
| >= | Greater or equal | 10 >= 10 returns true |
| < | Less than | 3 < 5 returns true |
| <= | Less or equal | 3 <= 5 returns true |

blocks.title: ⚠️ Important: === vs ==
blocks.content: Always use === and !== in JavaScript! The == operator can cause confusing bugs because it tries to convert types. For example, 0 == false is true (they're not the same type!).

blocks.title: Comparison Operators
blocks.code:
console.log(5 == 5);      // true
console.log(5 === 5);     // true
console.log(5 == '5');     // true (loose equality)
console.log(5 === '5');    // false (strict equality)

console.log(10 > 5);       // true
console.log(10 >= 10);     // true
console.log('apple' > 'Apple'); // true (capitals are 'smaller')

blocks.instruction: Check if 7 is strictly equal to the string '7':
blocks.explanation: === checks if values are equal and the same type. 7 and '7' are different types, so the result is true.

questions.question: What is the result of 5 === '5'?
questions.explanation: === checks both value and type. 5 is a number and '5' is a string, so they're not strictly equal.

questions.question: Which is safer to use in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: === is safer because it doesn't try to convert types. Use === to avoid unexpected bugs.
